extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system supports **real-time data exchange capabilities**, ensuring continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts with minimal latency. The framework enforces **low-overhead runtime configuration management**, enabling dynamic adjustments without full redeployment, which implies sub-second to millisecond-level synchronization for operational monitoring and control. Temporal granularity is aligned with **operational needs of the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) domain**, where device-level monitoring and control require high-frequency updates (e.g., sensor data polling at intervals ≤1 second for critical systems). Latency requirements are implicitly addressed through the framework’s **modular architecture with standardized extension points**, allowing custom services to adapt to specific time-scale demands (e.g., predictive analytics, anomaly detection, or real-time energy optimization).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system employs a **centralized-decentralized hybrid architecture**, where a **central DT Framework hub** coordinates multiple digital twin instances (e.g., per-building, per-floor, or per-zone granularity) while delegating operational responsibilities to specialized roles: **Building Control Engineers** (BIM-based device modeling), **Facility Managers** (real-time monitoring), and **DT Engineers** (service integration). The system supports **hierarchical multiplicities**, with each instance maintaining localized autonomy while adhering to global coordination via the central hub. Coordination mechanisms rely on **bidirectional data exchange** and **runtime configuration management**, enabling dynamic reallocation of responsibilities without full redeployment. The scope of each instance is defined by operational needs, with **modular extension points** allowing customization for specific use cases (e.g., energy management, occupancy analytics).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system follows a **service-oriented architecture (SOA) pattern**, orchestrated by a central **DT Framework hub** that facilitates **component integration** through standardized extension points. Orchestration is achieved via **coordinated roles**—Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers—each contributing to the DT lifecycle (e.g., deployment, monitoring, and service extension). Resource management is implicit in the **modular architecture**, where custom services and analytics are dynamically integrated without disrupting core functionality. System-wide coordination is enabled by **runtime configuration management**, allowing real-time adjustments to service priorities, data flows, and operational policies. The framework emphasizes **holistic building lifecycle management**, integrating enriched BIM models with operational data to create a unified digital representation.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system prioritizes **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** through adherence to **non-functional requirements (NF_X)**, including standardized data exchange protocols and interoperability frameworks. Integration with external systems is facilitated via **modular extension points**, enabling seamless connectivity with BIM tools (e.g., for device modeling), enterprise systems (e.g., facility management platforms), and other Digital Twins or infrastructure systems. Dependencies on external systems are managed through **low-overhead updates** and **runtime configuration**, ensuring scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure networks. The framework supports **enterprise-level integration patterns**, such as API-based communication and shared data models, to enable cross-system collaboration while maintaining autonomy for localized DT instances.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system employs a **model-driven deployment framework** that integrates **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** as the foundational data source, augmented to address its static limitations. The framework supports **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** through enriched BIM representations, enabling Building Control Engineers to define **sensors, actuators, and controllers** directly within the BIM environment. This creates a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent transformations via **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles**, extracting structured representations (e.g., device configurations, control logic) from enriched BIM models. Data sources include **real-time physical building data** synchronized via bidirectional exchange mechanisms, while **model relationships** are established through standardized ontologies and metadata schemas. The framework leverages **modular architecture** with **extension points** for custom services, ensuring scalability and adaptability across the Digital Twin lifecycle. Data management approaches include **automated artifact generation** (e.g., deployable DT components) with minimal manual intervention, and **interactive visualization interfaces** for monitoring and control. Key data components include:
  - **Device models** (sensors, actuators, controllers) embedded in BIM for semantic consistency.
  - **Control logic definitions** derived from BIM configurations.
  - **Real-time synchronization layers** for physical-to-digital data alignment.
  - **Customizable service plugins** integrated via standardized APIs for extended functionality (e.g., analytics, predictive maintenance).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The Digital Twin system supports **physical actuators and control mechanisms** primarily through **BIM-integrated device modeling**, enabling Building Control Engineers to define and configure actuators within the BIM environment. While specific **actuator types** (e.g., HVAC valves, motorized shades) are not detailed, the framework implies **remote control capabilities** via the Digital Twin interface, allowing Facility Managers to operate physical infrastructure. **Control logic** is implemented as part of the BIM-enriched models and deployed via the framework’s **MDE-generated artifacts**. **Actuation ranges and limitations** are implicitly constrained by the physical devices’ specifications, though exact ranges (e.g., valve opening percentages, motor speeds) are not provided. **Safety constraints** are likely enforced through the framework’s **runtime validation mechanisms** (e.g., error detection during deployment). **Addressing mechanisms** for actuators are inferred to rely on **semantic identifiers** embedded in BIM models, ensuring unambiguous mapping between digital and physical components. However, **specific protocols** (e.g., Modbus, OPC UA) or **remote control interfaces** (e.g., REST APIs, MQTT) are not explicitly mentioned in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system incorporates **physical sensing infrastructure** as part of the BIM-enriched models, where Building Control Engineers define **sensors** (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy sensors) within the BIM environment. **Sensor types** are not explicitly listed, but the framework implies support for **standardized Building Control System (BCS) sensors**. **Measurement capabilities** are derived from the sensors’ native specifications, though **spatial distribution** is constrained by the BIM’s geometric representations. **Sampling frequencies** and **data transmission rates** are not detailed, but the framework ensures **real-time data exchange** between physical sensors and the Digital Twin via bidirectional synchronization. **Accuracy and precision specifications** are not provided, but the system relies on **BIM-integrated metadata** to validate sensor data integrity. **Data collection mechanisms** are automated through the framework’s **MDE-driven extraction**, where sensor configurations are translated into deployable DT artifacts. **Data transmission protocols** (e.g., wireless, wired) are not specified, but the system emphasizes **minimal manual intervention** for sensor integration.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin framework addresses **model fidelity requirements** by overcoming BIM’s static limitations through **enriched semantic representations** and **MDE-based transformations**. **Validation and verification methods** include:
  - **Automated artifact generation** from BIM models to ensure consistency between digital and physical components.
  - **Runtime synchronization** mechanisms to detect discrepancies between the Digital Twin and physical system.
  - **Interactive visualization** for Facility Managers to monitor system states and validate operational accuracy.
  **Uncertainty sources** are not explicitly quantified, but the framework implies **error detection and correction** via runtime validation during deployment and operation. **Quality assurance processes** are embedded in the **modular architecture**, where custom services and plugins undergo **standardized extension points** for validation. However, **specific metrics** (e.g., fidelity thresholds, error tolerance levels) or **formal uncertainty quantification methods** (e.g., Bayesian inference, Monte Carlo simulations) are not mentioned in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin (DT) framework enables continuous bidirectional data exchange between physical buildings and their digital counterparts via **real-time data synchronization**. Key components include:

- **Data Types Transmitted**:
  Enriched Building Information Modeling (BIM) data (e.g., geometric, structural, and operational parameters), sensor telemetry (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy, energy consumption), and IoT device statuses (e.g., HVAC system states, lighting controls).

- **Communication Protocols**:
  Standardized protocols such as **OPC UA** (for industrial interoperability) and **MQTT** (for lightweight, event-driven sensor data transmission) are implied due to the framework’s emphasis on **heterogeneous system interoperability** (NF_X). Additional protocols may include **RESTful APIs** for structured data exchange with BIM tools (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD).

- **Transmission Frequencies**:
  **Continuous or near-real-time** (latency < 1 second for critical parameters like safety alarms) with configurable sampling rates for non-critical data (e.g., hourly for energy analytics). Event-triggered updates (e.g., threshold breaches in sensor readings) are prioritized over periodic polling.

- **Event Triggers**:
  Predefined thresholds (e.g., temperature > 30°C, occupancy > 90%), system alerts (e.g., equipment failures), and manual overrides (e.g., facility manager interventions) trigger immediate data ingestion into the DT. These events are processed via **runtime configuration management** to dynamically adjust data flow priorities.

- **Data Quality Assurance**:
  **Preprocessing steps** include:
  - **Anomaly detection** (e.g., statistical outliers in sensor data).
  - **Data validation** against BIM model schemas (e.g., IFC or COBie formats) to ensure consistency.
  - **Temporal alignment** of physical and digital timestamps via synchronized clocks (e.g., NTP synchronization for IoT devices).
  - **Redundancy checks** for critical data (e.g., cross-referencing multiple sensors for the same parameter).

- **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
  The framework employs a **modular architecture** with standardized extension points to integrate custom data pipelines (e.g., ETL processes for legacy systems). Data is aggregated in a **centralized hub** (DT Framework) that reconciles discrepancies between physical and digital states using **conflict resolution algorithms** (implied by NF_X scalability requirements). Integration adheres to **semantic interoperability** principles to align disparate data formats (e.g., converting IoT JSON payloads to BIM-compatible XML/JSON-LD).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this DT framework facilitates **control command execution** from the digital twin to physical building systems, ensuring operational alignment. Key technical aspects include:

- **Command Types**:
  **Real-time control commands** for:
  - **HVAC adjustments** (e.g., setpoint modifications, fan speed modulation).
  - **Lighting/occupancy management** (e.g., dynamic dimming based on sensor data).
  - **Equipment activation/deactivation** (e.g., start/stop for pumps, chillers).
  - **Safety interventions** (e.g., emergency shutdown protocols).
  **Predictive commands** (e.g., pre-cooling/heating based on weather forecasts) are enabled via integrated analytics services.

- **Control Message Formats**:
  Commands are encoded in **standardized formats** such as:
  - **OPC UA Information Models** for device-specific operations (e.g., BACnet/Modbus translations).
  - **JSON/XML payloads** for cross-platform compatibility (e.g., RESTful APIs to IoT gateways).
  - **Structured BIM extensions** (e.g., IFC4Addons for parametric control of building elements).

- **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
  Commands undergo **multi-layered validation**:
  1. **Semantic validation** against the DT’s operational schema (e.g., checking if a command aligns with the building’s BIM constraints).
  2. **Authorization checks** via role-based access control (e.g., *Facility Managers* vs. *DT Engineers*).
  3. **Cryptographic authentication** (e.g., TLS 1.3 for secure API calls, digital signatures for critical commands).
  Unauthorized or invalid commands are **blocked at the DT Framework’s edge layer**.

- **Feedback Systems**:
  **Closed-loop feedback** is implemented via:
  - **Acknowledgment messages** from physical devices (e.g., HTTP 200 OK for successful command execution).
  - **State updates** from sensors (e.g., confirming HVAC setpoint achievement).
  - **Error notifications** (e.g., timeouts, protocol failures) routed to the DT’s **runtime configuration manager** for adaptive retries or manual intervention.

- **Conflict Resolution**:
  Conflicts between digital commands and physical constraints (e.g., a command to lower temperature below the HVAC’s minimum) are resolved via:
  - **Priority-based arbitration** (e.g., safety commands override operational commands).
  - **Dynamic constraint propagation** from the BIM model (e.g., structural limits on load-bearing elements).
  - **Human-in-the-loop validation** for ambiguous cases (e.g., *Building Control Engineers* override automated decisions).

- **Command Execution Confirmation**:
  Confirmation is **timestamped and logged** in the DT’s audit trail, with **non-repudiation** ensured via blockchain-like append-only records (implied by NF_X maintainability requirements). Successful executions trigger **post-command analytics** (e.g., energy impact assessments).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for this DT framework is designed to support **low-latency, high-reliability communication** across heterogeneous systems, adhering to **NF_X interoperability and scalability** requirements. Key specifications include:

- **Communication Protocols**:
  - **Primary Protocols**:
    - **OPC UA** (for industrial automation interoperability, supporting secure data exchange over TCP/IP).
    - **MQTT/SN** (for IoT device connectivity, optimized for constrained networks with QoS levels 0–2).
    - **RESTful APIs** (for BIM tool integration, using HTTP/2 for multiplexed requests).
  - **Secondary Protocols**:
    - **BACnet/IP** (for legacy building automation systems).
    - **Modbus TCP** (for industrial sensors).
    - **CoAP** (for constrained edge devices with UDP-based communication).

- **Network Topology and Architecture**:
  A **hybrid peer-to-peer and centralized hub-and-spoke model**:
  - **Edge Layer**: IoT devices and local controllers (e.g., PLCs, gateways) communicate via **local area networks (LANs)** with **VLAN segmentation** for security.
  - **Core Layer**: The **DT Framework** acts as a **central hub**, aggregating data from edge nodes via **VPNs or private cloud connections**.
  - **Wide Area Network (WAN)**: Cloud-hosted components (if applicable) connect via **SD-WAN** for dynamic path optimization and redundancy.

- **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
  - **Critical Paths** (e.g., safety alarms, real-time control):
    - **Latency < 100ms** (target), achieved via **low-latency MQTT brokers** and **OPC UA PubSub** for multicast scenarios.
    - **Bandwidth**: ≥ **1 Mbps per 100 IoT devices** (scalable via QoS prioritization).
  - **Non-Critical Paths** (e.g., analytics, historical data):
    - **Latency < 1 second**, with **compression** (e.g., gzip for REST APIs) to reduce overhead.

- **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
  - **Network Redundancy**:
    - **Multi-path routing** (e.g., BGP for WAN failover).
    - **Load-balanced edge gateways** to prevent single points of failure.
  - **Data Redundancy**:
    - **Replicated databases** (e.g., PostgreSQL with synchronous replication) for the DT’s core data store.
    - **Checkpointing** of critical commands (e.g., every 5 seconds for HVAC setpoints).
  - **Fault Tolerance**:
    - **Automatic failover** to secondary brokers (e.g., MQTT mirroring) or cloud backups during edge node failures.

- **Security Protocols**:
  - **Encryption**:
    - **TLS 1.3** for all external communications (client-server and peer-to-peer).
    - **DTLS 1.2** for IoT device communications (UDP-based).
  - **Authentication**:
    - **OAuth 2.0/OIDC** for API access (JWT tokens with short expiry).
    - **X.509 certificates** for device authentication (OPC UA).
  - **Access Control**:
    - **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** integrated with the DT Framework’s **runtime configuration manager**.
    - **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** principles for edge devices (continuous authentication via MQTT QoS 2).
  - **Anomaly Detection**:
    - **Network intrusion detection** (e.g., Snort rules for OPC UA traffic patterns).
    - **Behavioral analytics** (e.g., anomaly detection in command frequencies).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **deployment infrastructure** for this Digital Twin framework supports **scalable, modular, and adaptable** hosting models to accommodate the **AECO domain’s dynamic operational needs**, with a focus on **NF_X scalability, maintainability, and accessibility**. Key components include:

- **Hosting Platforms**:
  - **Cloud-Based**:
    - **Public clouds** (e.g., AWS, Azure) for **scalable analytics and storage**, leveraging serverless functions (e.g., AWS Lambda) for event-driven processing.
    - **Hybrid cloud** deployments for **data sovereignty** (e.g., sensitive BIM data stored on-premise).
  - **Edge Computing**:
    - **Local gateways** (e.g., Raspberry Pi clusters or industrial PCs) for **low-latency processing** of IoT data (e.g., pre-filtering sensor streams).
    - **Fog nodes** (e.g., Cisco IOx) for intermediate aggregation of edge data before cloud transmission.
  - **On-Premise**:
    - **Dedicated servers** for **mission-critical DT instances** (e.g., safety-critical building systems) with **air-gapped networks** for maximum security.

- **Computational and Storage Requirements**:
  - **Compute**:
    - **Edge Layer**: ≤ **1 GHz CPU**, ≤ **512 MB RAM** per gateway (optimized for MQTT brokers and lightweight analytics).
    - **Core Layer**: **Multi-core CPUs (e.g., Intel Xeon or AMD EPYC)** with **≥ 16 GB RAM** for the DT Framework’s runtime environment (e.g., Java/Kotlin-based backends).
    - **Cloud Layer**: **GPU-accelerated instances** (e.g., NVIDIA T4) for **machine learning-based predictions** (e.g., energy demand forecasting).
  - **Storage**:
    - **Operational Data**: **SSD-backed databases** (e.g., TimescaleDB for time-series sensor data) with **hot/warm/cold tiering**.
    - **Historical Data**: **Object storage** (e.g., S3-compatible) for long-term archives (compressed via **Parquet/ORC formats**).
    - **BIM Models**: **Centralized repository** (e.g., Autodesk Forge or proprietary IFC databases) with **version control** (e.g., Git LFS for large files).

- **Scalability Mechanisms**:
  - **Horizontal Scaling**:
    - **Containerization** (e.g., Docker/Kubernetes) for the DT Framework’s microservices (e.g., separate pods for BIM integration, analytics, and control).
    - **Auto-scaling policies** (e.g., Kubernetes HPA) based on **MQTT message backlog** or **CPU utilization thresholds**.
  - **Vertical Scaling**:
    - **Database sharding** for sensor data (e.g., partitioning by building zone).
    - **Elastic compute** (e.g., AWS EC2 Spot Instances) for non-critical workloads.
  - **Modular Extensibility**:
    - **Plug-and-play services** via **standardized APIs** (e.g., OpenAPI/Swagger) and **event-driven architectures** (e.g., Apache Kafka for inter-service communication).

- **Deployment Models Supported**:
  - **Single-Tenant**: Isolated DT instances for **proprietary building management systems**.
  - **Multi-Tenant**: Shared cloud infrastructure with **tenant isolation** (e.g., Kubernetes namespaces) for **utility-scale deployments** (e.g., smart city initiatives).
  - **Hybrid**: Combines on-premise core systems with cloud-based analytics (e.g., **AWS Outposts** for edge cloud).

- **Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
  - **High Availability (HA)**:
    - **Multi-AZ deployments** (e.g., AWS RDS Multi-AZ) for databases.
    - **Active-active replication** for the DT Framework’s core services.
  - **Disaster Recovery (DR)**:
    - **Geographically distributed backups** (e.g., cross-region replication).
    - **Point-in-time recovery** for databases (e.g., PostgreSQL WAL archiving).
  - **Uptime SLAs**:
    - **≥ 99.95% availability** for critical components (e.g., control systems).
    - **99.9% for analytics services** (with scheduled maintenance windows).
  - **Zero-Downtime Updates**:
    - **Blue-green deployments** for the DT Framework’s runtime components.
    - **Canary releases** for new service integrations (e.g., gradual rollout of AI-based predictive maintenance).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) domain spans multiple phases, including **conceptualization, design, construction, operation, and decommissioning**, each requiring distinct representation types and transition management. During **conceptualization**, DTs are initialized with **BIM (Building Information Modeling) models** as the foundational digital representation, integrating geometric and semantic data. In the **design phase**, DTs evolve through **modular construction frameworks**, where parametric and generative design tools (e.g., Grasshopper, Dynamo) are employed to refine asset configurations. **Transition management** between phases relies on **data synchronization protocols** (e.g., IFC, COBie) to ensure consistency, though fragmented schemas and lack of standardized workflows remain challenges. **Lifecycle governance** is fragmented across stakeholders, with **limited unified frameworks** for asset management, leading to reliance on **case-specific deployments** rather than scalable, modular solutions. The **operation phase** emphasizes real-time synchronization via **sensor networks and IoT**, while **decommissioning** requires **data archival and legacy system integration**, often lacking standardized procedures. Governance approaches are ad-hoc, with **insufficient stakeholder alignment** and **high implementation costs** hindering adoption of lifecycle-aware DT systems.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process in AECO Digital Twins involves **methodologies rooted in BIM integration, workflow automation, and bidirectional communication** between physical and digital assets. **Development methodologies** include **modular construction approaches** (e.g., prefabrication, prefabricated components) to enable scalable DT implementations, though these require **customized automation scripts** due to limited out-of-the-box support. **Quality assurance (QA)** practices are primarily **case-specific**, relying on manual validation of data consistency across BIM and sensor inputs. **Testing and validation** are often **ad-hoc**, with no standardized protocols for verifying DT accuracy, leading to **high operational risks** in real-time monitoring. **Version control** is poorly defined, with **no explicit mention of versioning tools** (e.g., Git, SVN) in the context of DT evolution. **Evolution management** is constrained by **fragmented data schemas** and **lack of orchestration frameworks**, requiring manual intervention to update DTs with new sensor data or design changes. Frameworks like **Bentley’s iTwin.js** offer partial automation but demand **substantial manual effort** due to proprietary dependencies and **limited interoperability** with non-AECO standards.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Digital Twin systems in AECO generate **real-time and predictive insights** through integration with **artificial intelligence (AI), sensor networks, and analytics engines**, though these capabilities are **not fully automated** or standardized. **Types of insights** include **operational efficiency metrics** (e.g., energy consumption, structural health monitoring), **cost modeling predictions**, and **life cycle management analytics**. **Analytics and processing** rely on **custom-built pipelines** due to the absence of unified frameworks, with **machine learning models** often deployed post-hoc for anomaly detection or predictive maintenance. **Decision-making processes** are **stakeholder-dependent**, with **limited decision support tools** embedded in DT systems. User interaction methods include **proprietary dashboards** (e.g., iTwin’s visualization tools) and **manual data queries**, but **confidence measures for recommendations** are not explicitly defined, leading to **low trust in automated suggestions**. The lack of **confidence quantification** (e.g., uncertainty intervals, reliability scores) exacerbates challenges in **real-time decision-making**.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Standardization in AECO Digital Twin systems is **fragmented**, with **no unified compliance frameworks** governing implementation. **Relevant industry standards** include **BIM-specific schemas** (e.g., **IFC 4.0**, **COBie 2.1**) for geometric and semantic interoperability, but **adoption remains inconsistent** due to **proprietary ecosystem dependencies** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin). **Certification requirements** are **not standardized**, with **no widely recognized DT certification programs** in AECO. **Compliance frameworks** are **case-specific**, relying on **ad-hoc integration of IoT protocols** (e.g., MQTT, OPC UA) without a **unified interoperability standard**. **Interoperability challenges** arise from **lack of bidirectional communication protocols**, forcing manual data mapping between BIM, sensor networks, and analytics tools. Open-source frameworks like **Eclipse Ditto** and **BaSyx** offer **standard compliance** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) but require **significant manual configuration**, limiting usability in AECO. The absence of **modular, plug-and-play standards** hinders scalable DT adoption, with **proprietary lock-in** (e.g., iTwin’s ecosystem) further complicating interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The provided documents do not explicitly detail specific security and safety measures (e.g., cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, or authentication systems) for Digital Twin systems. However, the context implies critical considerations in DT implementations:

1. **Runtime Adaptability and Semantic Coherence**: Lehner et al.’s survey [48] highlights the need for robust mechanisms to maintain model integrity, which inherently requires safeguarding against unauthorized modifications or data breaches during runtime. This suggests implicit reliance on secure model synchronization and validation protocols to prevent semantic drift (as discussed in Abbasi et al. [49]), though explicit security frameworks are not described.

2. **Tool Interoperability and Governance**: The emphasis on Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) [35, 36, 40] implies that formalized governance models—including compliance with organizational policies—are encoded into the DT system. While not explicitly stated, such governance likely includes access controls and audit trails to ensure data integrity and confidentiality, aligning with safety-critical system considerations in complex domains like AECO.

3. **Stakeholder Coordination Challenges**: The documents [33, 34] mention difficulties in stakeholder engagement and data integration, which may introduce vulnerabilities if not managed through secure collaboration frameworks (e.g., role-based access control, encrypted data sharing). However, no specific technologies (e.g., OAuth, blockchain, or zero-trust architectures) are referenced.

4. **Fail-Safe Mechanisms**: The focus on real-time data utilization and visualization [33, 34] implies the need for fail-safe mechanisms to handle data corruption or cyber threats, though no technical specifications (e.g., redundancy protocols, intrusion detection systems) are provided.

No concrete details on threat mitigation strategies, risk assessment approaches, or safety-critical system specifications (e.g., ISO 27001, NIST frameworks) are mentioned in the provided documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific

Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.
block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS
block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.
block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
